title: Hydration Only Matters on Workout Days
hook: Hydration only matters on workout days.
science_text: Hydration is an every-single-day habit. Focus on fluids all day, not just around workouts — that's how you show up ready.
source_citation: Hydrate Early and Often (CPSDA / National Dairy Council)

title: Small Sweat Loss Hurts Performance
hook: Losing even a little sweat weight can hurt your performance.
science_text: Losing just a small percentage of your body weight in sweat measurably slows you down. Staying topped up keeps your speed and endurance where they belong.
source_citation: Hydrate Early and Often (CPSDA / National Dairy Council)

title: Wait Until You're Thirsty to Drink
hook: Waiting until you're thirsty is a solid hydration strategy.
science_text: Thirst shows up late to the game. By the time you feel it, you're already behind. Hydrate early and often — before, during, and after exercise.
source_citation: Hydrate Early and Often (CPSDA / National Dairy Council)

title: Rehydrate Within 2 Hours After Exercise
hook: You should rehydrate within a couple hours after exercise.
science_text: The clock starts when training ends. Replacing your fluid losses within about 2 hours sets up your recovery and tomorrow's performance.
source_citation: Hydrate Early and Often (CPSDA / National Dairy Council)

title: You Need Sports Drinks for Every Workout
hook: You need a sports drink for every workout.
science_text: For sessions under an hour, water does the job perfectly. Sports drinks earn their place during long, intense, sweaty training.
source_citation: What's In Your Sweat? (SportsRD.org)

title: Electrolytes Matter All Day, Not Just Workouts
hook: Electrolytes should be part of your whole day, not just your workout.
science_text: Water and electrolytes consumed throughout the day keep your fluid and electrolyte levels optimal — so you start every session ready to go.
source_citation: What's In Your Sweat? (SportsRD.org)

title: Sweat Is Just Water
hook: Sweat is just water leaving your body.
science_text: Sweat carries sodium, chloride, potassium, magnesium, and calcium out with it. That's why hard-sweating athletes replace more than just water.
source_citation: What's In Your Sweat? (SportsRD.org)

title: Meals Are Part of Training
hook: Your meals are part of your training, just like reps in the gym.
science_text: Every plate drives your energy, hydration, and recovery. Fuel with purpose and every practice gets better.
source_citation: Building a Performance Plate (NCAA / SCAN / CPSDA)

title: Hard Training Makes Up for Poor Fueling
hook: Training hard can make up for poor fueling.
science_text: You can't out-train a bad diet. Without proper fueling, your full athletic potential stays locked away — no matter how many hours you grind.
source_citation: Building a Performance Plate (NCAA / SCAN / CPSDA)

title: Your Plate Stays the Same All Year
hook: Your plate should look the same in-season and offseason.
science_text: Your needs change with your training. In-season and hard days call for more energy foods; offseason and light days shift toward more fruits, veggies, and protein.
source_citation: Building a Performance Plate (NCAA / SCAN / CPSDA)

title: Fruits & Veggies Are Your Antioxidant Defense
hook: Fruits and vegetables are your plate's antioxidant defense squad.
science_text: Berries, spinach, broccoli, carrots, and friends deliver antioxidants that power recovery and keep your immune system game-ready.
source_citation: Building a Performance Plate (NCAA / SCAN / CPSDA)

title: Skipping Breakfast Doesn't Affect Performance
hook: Skipping breakfast saves time and doesn't affect performance.
science_text: Skipping breakfast leaves you tired and sluggish, then ravenous at night — which wrecks your sleep and tomorrow's appetite. Break the cycle with morning fuel.
source_citation: Eating Frequency for the Student-Athlete (NCAA / ASPDA)

title: A Big Meal Before Bed Helps Tomorrow
hook: A big meal right before bed sets you up for a great morning.
science_text: Late-night feasts make it harder to fall asleep and kill your morning appetite. Spread your fuel through the day and both your sleep and breakfast improve.
source_citation: Eating Frequency for the Student-Athlete (NCAA / ASPDA)

title: A Balanced Meal Combines Protein, Carbs & Veggies
hook: A balanced meal combines lean protein, carbs, and fruits or vegetables.
science_text: That's the winning formula: turkey sub with pretzels and fruit, pasta with meat sauce and salad, or chicken stir-fry with rice and veggies. Balanced and built to perform.
source_citation: Eating Frequency for the Student-Athlete (NCAA / ASPDA)

title: Flexibility Matters When Days Don't Go as Planned
hook: Flexibility matters — some days won't go as planned, and that's okay.
science_text: Some days are busier than others. Stay flexible, focus on meeting your overall needs, and get back on your routine at the next meal. Progress beats perfection.
source_citation: Eating Frequency for the Student-Athlete (NCAA / ASPDA)

title: One Hour of Prep Upgrades Your Whole Week
hook: One hour a week of shopping and food prep can upgrade your entire week of fueling.
science_text: Fail to plan, plan to fail. One planned hour of shopping and prep positively impacts every food choice you make all week — that's a competitive advantage.
source_citation: Fueling To Win: Smart Shopping Strategies (SportsRD.org)

title: Frozen Veggies Are Less Nutritious Than Fresh
hook: Frozen vegetables are less nutritious than fresh ones.
science_text: Frozen veggies are just as nutrient-packed as fresh, they're fast and easy, and they never go bad in the back of the fridge. Stock up proudly.
source_citation: Fueling To Win: Smart Shopping Strategies (SportsRD.org)

title: Shop Hungry to Discover New Foods
hook: Shopping while hungry is a great way to discover new foods.
science_text: Hungry shopping = splurge shopping. Eat first, bring your list, and stick to it. Discipline at the store shows up on your plate.
source_citation: Fueling To Win: Smart Shopping Strategies (SportsRD.org)

title: If It's On Sale, Buy It
hook: If it's on sale, it belongs in your cart.
science_text: A deal on food you don't need is still money and nutrition lost. Don't rationalize the sale — run your list like a game plan.
source_citation: Fueling To Win: Smart Shopping Strategies (SportsRD.org)

title: Convenience Foods Can Be Worth the Cost
hook: Convenience foods like pre-cut veggies and rotisserie chicken can be worth the extra cost.
science_text: Weigh the cost of convenience: if pre-cut veggies, hard-boiled eggs, or a rotisserie chicken get healthy food into you faster, that's money well spent.
source_citation: Fueling To Win: Smart Shopping Strategies (SportsRD.org)

title: Fuel for Expected Work, Refuel for Work Done
hook: Before training you fuel for the work you EXPECT to do; after, you fuel for the work you DID do.
science_text: Pre-fuel matches the session ahead — easy day, balanced snack; hard day, carb-focused. Afterward, your recovery plate matches what you actually did. That's fueling with intention.
source_citation: Macronutrient Timing & Periodization (ASPDA)

title: Training on Empty Makes You Tougher
hook: Training on empty makes you tougher and fitter.
science_text: Skipping fuel before high-intensity training blunts your aerobic gains over time and risks low energy availability. Always pre-fuel your hard sessions — that's how you actually get faster.
source_citation: Macronutrient Timing & Periodization (ASPDA)

title: Timing Matters as Much as What You Eat
hook: WHEN you eat matters, not just WHAT you eat.
science_text: Within-day energy balance — matching your intake to your training through the day — is a key performance factor. Same food, better timing, better results.
source_citation: Macronutrient Timing & Periodization (ASPDA)

title: Rest Days Deserve Fuel Too
hook: Rest days deserve fuel too.
science_text: Energy needs spread across your whole week — including rest days — to support recovery and keep your energy availability strong. Rest days are when you rebuild.
source_citation: Macronutrient Timing & Periodization (ASPDA)

title: Long Training Fuel Keeps Blood Sugar Stable
hook: The goal of fueling during long training is keeping your blood sugar stable.
science_text: Steady blood glucose = steady energy = steady performance. That's why endurance athletes fuel by the hour during long sessions.
source_citation: Macronutrient Timing & Periodization (ASPDA)
